title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
tex_block_html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_2 -- "AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_2_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (statement)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

\begin{Lem}[AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}). Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ -- i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous -- such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.
\end{Lem}

\end{document}

Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def def-acylic) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def def-topological-order); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ – i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous – such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def def_3_5, item i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}). Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ -- i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous -- such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof lifted verbatim from the LN at lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:228-244.
\begin{proof}
($\Leftarrow$)
Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$.
If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$),
then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$)
Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph.
Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$
and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$.
Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$
(otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle).
Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$.
If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$---unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg). Then $G$ is acyclic (in the sense of def def-acylic) if and only if there exists a topological order of $G$ (in the sense of def def-topological-order); equivalently, $G$ is acyclic if and only if there exists a strict total order $<$ on $J \cup V$ – i.e.\ a binary relation $<$ on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous – such that, for every $v, w \in J \cup V$, $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$ (using the parent-set notation $\Pa^G$ of def def_3_5, item i.). The label set $L$ plays no role on either side of the biconditional.

Proof.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$. If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$), then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph. Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$ and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$. Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$ (otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle). Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$. If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$—unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.

lean_blocks.explanation: This single `variable` line is statement-typing infrastructure rather than content. In Lean 4 a `variable` declaration auto-binds the listed implicits into every subsequent declaration in the namespace that mentions them, so the `theorem` below picks up `Node` and `[DecidableEq Node]` without restating them. The `Type*` annotation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, matching the convention inherited from `def_3_1` (`CDMG.lean`). The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is load-bearing for the theorem signature in three separate places: through `def_3_2`'s `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance, which is what lets `v ∈ G` typecheck inside the topological-order quantifiers; through `def_3_5`'s `G.Pa w` parent-set in the parent-precedence clause of `G.IsTopologicalOrder`; and through the `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` recursion in `def_3_4` that `G.IsAcyclic` (from `def_3_6`) quantifies over. Stronger fixtures like `[Fintype Node]` are deliberately avoided at the statement level — the proof body may want them locally, but they would over-commit the signature.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-bind `Node` (universe-polymorphic) and `DecidableEq` for `Membership`, `Pa`, and `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
theorem acyclic_iff_topological_order (G : CDMG Node) :
    G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt
lean_blocks.explanation: This is the first **theorem** on the website (every earlier row was a definition). It captures the equivalence between the global acyclicity condition — no non-trivial directed walk from any vertex of $J \cup V$ back to itself, the `G.IsAcyclic` predicate from `def_3_6` — and the *existence* of a topological order, i.e. some strict total order on $J \cup V$ that makes every parent precede each of its children. The biconditional `↔` shape lets downstream callers cite either direction by `.mp` or `.mpr`: the `⇒` direction (acyclic gives a topological order) is the constructive heart of the LN proof and is the workhorse projection later chapters reach for when they want a parent-first traversal; the `⇐` direction is the easier pigeonhole that a topological order kills directed cycles by trichotomy and irreflexivity. The right-hand side reads `∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` because a CDMG generally admits many topological orders (any linear refinement of the parent-precedence partial order qualifies) and the LN never designates a canonical one — `G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` is the 4-clause predicate (irreflexivity, transitivity, trichotomy, parent-precedes) from `def_3_8`. The proof, sitting outside the marker tags, runs `⇒` by building a partial order from walk reachability and invoking Mathlib's Szpilrajn `extend_partialOrder` for a linear extension, and `⇐` by walking the directed cycle by trichotomy of `lt` to a contradiction with irreflexivity. The name `acyclic_iff_topological_order` follows Mathlib's `…_iff_…` naming convention for biconditional lemmas.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Theorem: acyclicity of a CDMG is equivalent to the existence of a topological order on its nodes.
theorem acyclic_iff_topological_order (G : CDMG Node) :
    -- `↔` exposes both directions via `.mp`/`.mpr`; `∃` because many topological orders exist and the LN picks none.
    G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt

design_choices:
**Typing context inherited from prior rows.** The surrounding `variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` sits in a sibling three-dash `--- helper` marker block, matching the convention used throughout `Chapter3_GraphTheory/Section3_1/`: two-dash markers wrap declarations whose body is the formalised LN content of the row, while three-dash markers carry the implicit typing infrastructure their signatures reference. Both fixtures are load-bearing here. `Node : Type*` is the ambient vertex carrier — the silent universe of vertex labels that the LN's $J \cup V$ is drawn from — while `[DecidableEq Node]` is reached through by the `Finset`-backed membership tests of `def_3_2`'s `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance (so that the LN's literal "for every $v, w \in J \cup V$" transports verbatim onto `v ∈ G`), by the `Walk` machinery of `def_3_4` that `G.IsAcyclic` from `def_3_6` recurses over, and by the `G.Pa w` parent-set construction of `def_3_5` that the topological-order predicate references. Stronger structure on `Node` (no `Fintype`, no `LinearOrder`) is deliberately *not* requested at this layer — the proof body may want `Fintype` locally to enumerate `(G.J ∪ G.V).toList`, but those local needs are kept from leaking into the surface signature, and the `Finset`-valued `G.J`, `G.V` already supply finiteness on the domain the order must be total over.

**Biconditional `↔` rather than two separate one-direction theorems.** The LN's "if and only if" lands as a single `Iff`, packaging both directions in one ground-truth lemma. Splitting into `acyclic_implies_topological_order` and `topological_order_implies_acyclic` was considered and rejected. The LN treats this as one claim; every downstream consumer needs only one direction at a time and reaches it via `.mp` or `.mpr` at zero syntactic cost; and bundling keeps the LN-to-Lean cross-reference grep-able under a single name. The `.mp` direction (acyclic ⟹ a topological order exists) is the workhorse projection for downstream chapters that thread a parent-first traversal over an ADMG — `IsADMG` carries `IsAcyclic` as its first conjunct in `CDMGTypes.lean`. The `.mpr` direction (topological order ⟹ acyclic) is the certificate route for causal-discovery algorithms that construct an ordering and need to read off acyclicity.

**Existential `∃ lt : Node → Node → Prop, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` over a bare relation, not a bundled order structure.** Forced by `def_3_8`'s upstream design: its `IsTopologicalOrder` is a property of an external `lt : Node → Node → Prop`, and its "Downstream consumers" design block literally anticipates this row's existential shape. Four bundled alternatives were considered and rejected. (a) A function `topologicalOrder : G → (Node → Node → Prop)` that picks one canonically — wrong semantic shape, because a CDMG generally admits *many* topological orders (any linear extension of the parent partial order qualifies) and the LN never picks one. (b) `∃ lt : LinearOrder Node, …` — Mathlib's `LinearOrder` forces totality, transitivity, and antisymmetry on the *full* `Node` type, excluding perfectly valid orders that leave nodes outside $J \cup V$ unrelated. (c) A `Nonempty (TopologicalOrder G)` structure or a `Σ lt, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` package — both inject either uniqueness or an extractable canonical witness the LN never commits to. (d) A subtype-restricted `∃ lt : StrictOrder ↥(G.J ∪ G.V), …` — avoids over-commitment but forces every consumer to coerce nodes across the `↥(G.J ∪ G.V)` boundary at every use site. The existential over a bare relation is the right semantic shape; `Classical.choice` is the API surface for downstream consumers that need to fix one.

**Proof strategy: walk-reachability partial order plus Szpilrajn.** The forward direction follows the LN's idea — there is a partial order to extract — but takes a semantically equivalent route to the LN's hand-rolled inductive parent-less-pick construction. We define $\mathrm{le}_0\ u\ v := u = v \lor \exists\, p : \text{Walk } G\ u\ v,\ p.\text{IsDirectedWalk} \land p.\text{length} \ge 1$, the reflexive walk-reachability relation on `Node`. Reflexivity is trivial, transitivity is walk concatenation (`Walk.comp`), and antisymmetry is acyclicity: if directed walks $a \to b$ and $b \to a$ both existed, concatenating them produces a non-trivial directed walk $a \to a$, contradicting `G.IsAcyclic` at $a$. We register `IsPreorder` and `IsPartialOrder` instances and invoke Mathlib's Szpilrajn theorem `extend_partialOrder` (a.k.a. `Order.extend_partialOrder` / `IsPartialOrder.toLinearOrder`) to obtain a linear order `s` extending $\mathrm{le}_0$. The strict version `lt u v := s u v ∧ u ≠ v` then satisfies the four conjuncts of `IsTopologicalOrder`: irreflexivity from `u ≠ v`, transitivity from `s`'s transitivity (with the `≠` clause closed via `s`'s antisymmetry), trichotomy from `s`'s totality, and parent-precedes from the chain `(u, v) ∈ G.E ⟹ Walk.singleEdge_directedWalk ⟹ le₀ u v ⟹ s u v` together with a self-loop ruling out `u = v` via acyclicity. The backward direction is the easy one — a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \to v_1 \to \cdots \to v_n = v$ would give $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$ under any topological order, contradicting irreflexivity. This is packaged as the private helper `Walk.lt_of_directedWalk_pos`, which walks along edges using parent-precedes and transitivity.

**Mathlib re-use: Szpilrajn, `IsPartialOrder`, and the strict-from-non-strict conversion.** None at the statement signature level — the existential binds a bare `Node → Node → Prop` rather than a Mathlib `LinearOrder` for the over-commitment reasons above. Inside the proof, the heavy lifting is done by `extend_partialOrder` (Mathlib's constructive Szpilrajn extension producing a linear order from any partial order, which Lean treats as `IsLinearOrder Node s` together with a containment witness `le₀ ≤ s`) and by the typeclass machinery `Std.Refl` / `IsTrans` / `Std.Antisymm` / `IsPreorder` / `IsPartialOrder` registered with `haveI` so resolution can find them. The strict order is read off as `s u v ∧ u ≠ v` — the canonical idiom for converting a non-strict linear order into its strict companion. These are the right hooks because Szpilrajn is precisely the abstract content of the LN's enumeration argument: an acyclic directed graph induces a partial order (walk reachability), and a partial order extends to a linear order. Alternatives — building the linear extension by hand via a `Finset` recursion picking parent-less elements one at a time, mirroring the LN literally — would be 50+ lines of `Finset` plumbing reproducing what Szpilrajn already proves abstractly, with no semantic gain.

**Relationship to the LN's inductive construction.** The LN proof picks $v_1, \dots, v_K$ by repeatedly extracting a parent-less node from the induced subgraph on the remaining vertices and reads off the order. We package the same underlying partial order (walk reachability) and invoke Szpilrajn to do the enumeration. The two constructions land in the same logical statement and produce the same *kind* of witness: any topological order qualifies; neither the LN nor this Leanification picks a canonical one. This is a semantic re-implementation, not a literal transcription — appropriate because the LN itself never relies on the specific order produced, only on the bare existence claim.

**Naming `acyclic_iff_topological_order`.** Standard Mathlib `snake_case_iff_snake_case` convention for biconditional lemmas: the LHS predicate, the connective `iff`, and the RHS noun phrase, all in lower snake case. The theorem lives inside `namespace CDMG` (matching every sibling chapter-3 result), so the dot-projected use site reads `(claim : G.IsAcyclic).mp` or `G.acyclic_iff_topological_order.mpr` and the unqualified surface name remains `acyclic_iff_topological_order`. Labels `L` are correctly absent from both sides — acyclicity and topological order are properties of the $(J, V, E)$-skeleton alone — and the name reflects that.

**Non-canonical witness — intentional, not an API gap.** The `∃` deliberately does not designate a canonical topological order. Many generally exist (every linear extension of the parent-precedence partial order qualifies), and the LN never picks one. There is no companion `def_topological_order_of_acyclic : G.IsAcyclic → (Node → Node → Prop)` in this row, and that absence is deliberate. Downstream theorems that need to fix a specific order must either pass `lt` as an explicit hypothesis or `Classical.choice`-extract it locally at the use site (inheriting the non-canonicality from the existential). This matches the LN's treatment and keeps the row faithful to a claim about *existence*, not selection.
